front: What is the main purpose of the `static` keyword in Java?
back: The `static` keyword is used to create members (variables and methods) that belong to the class itself, rather than to any specific instance of the class. This means they can be accessed without creating an object of the class.

front: How do you access a static variable or method?
back: Static members are accessed using the class name followed by the dot operator, e.g., `ClassName.staticVariable` or `ClassName.staticMethod()`. They can also be accessed via an object reference, but this is discouraged as it can be misleading.

front: Can a static method directly access instance variables or call non-static methods? Why or why not?
back: No. A static method cannot directly access instance variables or call non-static methods because they exist only when an object is instantiated. A static method exists and can be called without any instances of the class being created.

front: What is a common use case for static variables?
back: A common use is for constants (declared as `static final`) or for shared data that should be common to all instances of a class, such as a counter that tracks the number of objects created.

front: What is a static block and when is it executed?
back: A static block, defined as `static { ... }`, is used for static initialization of a class. It is executed only once when the class is loaded into memory by the JVM, before any objects are created or any static methods are called.
